claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, could you tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who's 13, and Blake, who's 15.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have Alex (13) and Blake (15). 

Let me ask you: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And do both Alex and Blake live with you in your home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since Blake doesn't live with you, let me ask: **Do you make any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep?** This could include things like:
- Money for clothes, food, or pocket money
- Financial contributions for their accommodation
- Regular payments for their maintenance

And if so, how much do you contribute and how often?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Blake's upkeep. I help with things like clothes and other expenses - I give about £26 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Now I need to understand a bit more about Blake's living situation:

**How long has Blake been living away from you? Has it been more or less than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about a month or so - less than 8 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now let me ask about their education and work status:

**Are Alex and Blake both currently in approved education (like school or college), or are either of them working or in training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both just in regular school - Alex is in secondary school and Blake is still in school too, just year 11.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment:

**Do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 per year?** 

(This won't affect your eligibility, but it may mean you need to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not get into that right now - I'm mainly just trying to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for my children first.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine! I have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

**For Alex:** Since Alex is 13 years old, lives with you in the UK, and is in regular secondary school, you clearly meet the eligibility criteria.

**For Blake:** Even though Blake doesn't live with you, you're still eligible because you're contributing £26 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which is more than the standard Child Benefit rate (£25.60/week). This means you're considered responsible for Blake under the rules. Additionally, since Blake has only been living away for about a month (less than 8 weeks), you would typically continue receiving Child Benefit during this period anyway.

Both children are under 16 and in approved education, so you meet all the requirements. If you haven't already claimed, you can proceed with your Child Benefit application!

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and is in regular secondary school education. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Alex meets all these criteria, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16), is still in school (year 11), and you are contributing towards Blake's upkeep. Although Blake doesn't live with you, you are contributing approximately £26 per week towards Blake's expenses. Under the rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (for example on food, clothes or pocket money). The current Child Benefit rate for one child is £25.60 per week, and you're contributing £26 per week, which exceeds this amount. Additionally, Blake has been living away for less than 8 weeks, and during the first 8 weeks you would usually continue to get Child Benefit anyway if nobody else claims. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for both children. Alex qualifies because they are under 16, live with you in the UK, and are in education. Blake qualifies because although they don't live with you, you are contributing at least as much as the Child Benefit amount (£26/week vs £25.60/week standard rate) towards their upkeep, which makes you responsible for them under the rules. Both children are in approved education and under 16 years old.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and is in regular secondary school education. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Alex meets all these criteria, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16), is still in school (year 11), and you are contributing towards Blake's upkeep. Although Blake doesn't live with you, you are contributing approximately £26 per week towards Blake's expenses. Under the rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (for example on food, clothes or pocket money). The current Child Benefit rate for one child is £25.60 per week, and you're contributing £26 per week, which exceeds this amount. Additionally, Blake has been living away for less than 8 weeks, and during the first 8 weeks you would usually continue to get Child Benefit anyway if nobody else claims. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for both children. Alex qualifies because they are under 16, live with you in the UK, and are in education. Blake qualifies because although they don't live with you, you are contributing at least as much as the Child Benefit amount (£26/week vs £25.60/week standard rate) towards their upkeep, which makes you responsible for them under the rules. Both children are in approved education and under 16 years old.
